summary: A bibliography page from PhilPapers featuring a brief biographical overview of Friedrich Nietzsche and a collection of abstracts from academic papers analyzing his philosophy. The abstracts cover his critiques of Platonism and Christianity, his concepts of the 'free spirit' and 'amor fati', and his use of metaphors like the subterranean man and the molting snake.

principles.rationale: The theological, metaphysical, and moral foundations of Western civilization have lost their absolute authority, ushering in the problem of nihilism.

principles.statement: There is no 'doer' behind the 'deed'; the action itself is everything.
principles.rationale: The idea of a separate, underlying subject is an illusion; human beings are better understood as a hierarchy of drives, and agency is found entirely in the action.

frameworks.name: Revaluation of All Values (Umwerthung aller Werthe)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When addressing modern cultural decadence and the collapse of traditional moral frameworks.
steps: Criticize and devaluate regnant moral values.
Imaginatively reinterpret these values while preserving their affective vitality.
Alter attitudes through both rational and arational (rhetorical) means to secure change in dominant values.

mental_models.name: The Snake's Molting (Häutung)
mental_models.description: A metaphor for intellectual self-transformation. A thinker writes to rid themselves of their current thoughts, leaving them behind like a rigid skin to start a new life.
mental_models.example: Nietzsche views his own writing process as an experience of self-transformation, where the published works are merely the 'rigid skin left by the animal.'

mental_models.name: The Subterranean Man
mental_models.description: A metaphor for a method of critique that involves going underneath established epistemological and moral grounds to undermine traditional notions.
mental_models.example: Nietzsche uses this image in the preface to Daybreak to describe his deep, excavating critique of morality, acting as a 'subterranean man' at work.

signature_quotes.context: His most famous declaration regarding the state of theology and metaphysics.

signature_quotes.text: as a disciple of Dionysus, I would rather be a satyr than a saint.
signature_quotes.context: Stated in Ecce Homo, reflecting his preference for earthly, affirmative existence over ascetic holiness.
Used to elucidate a view of agency that rejects the separation of the 'doer' from the 'deed.'
From the preface to Daybreak, describing his method of philosophical critique.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on rational dialectics and Platonic transcendence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It attempts to keep the agonal and erotic drives strictly under the control of reason, which repeats a metaphysical closure and ignores the movement of life.

anti_patterns.description: Adhering to Christian ethics and ascetic ideals.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is an expression of 'slave morality' and decadence that suppresses vitality and ultimately becomes nihilistic when it can no longer confer meaning on life.
